item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article was missing

« SCP-2920 | SCP-2921 | SCP-2922 »

I added it in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Who knows, maybe they've produced a 'dead in all possible timelines' gun.

Something like SCP-3797, perhaps? ;) It's not probabilistic, but the way it works means that if you use it, the target will be 100% dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've modified the wording for clarity to appease both gods

I'm playing both sides, that way I always come out on top

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, I like that line for the exact opposite reason — just like SCP-2719 reveals a lot about the nature of metaphysical anomalies, this implies the Foundation is deeply involved with probabilistic anomalies. Who knows, maybe they've produced a 'dead in all possible timelines' gun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Termination methods used must possess a 0% chance of failure.

Quibble with this, because the Foundation literally can't reach 0% exactly. Absolutely any method you can use to kill someone has to have a potential failure state of some probability, for any of a variety of reasons. Maybe the mechanism fails to operate properly. Maybe the method used isn't effective somehow. Maybe the individual has a random heretofore-unknown resistance to the method. Maybe the stars aligned and freak good luck keeps the method from being fatal (history is rife with stories of "how could they possibly have survived that?"). That last one in particular would be impossible to control unless the Foundation can literally control probability, and that's a power it really shouldn't be allowed to have.

But I think this opens up an avenue of creativity. What would the Foundation deem acceptably close to zero to be effective containment? How would they determine that? I think that would be a solid addition, especially since you have an object that deals with theoretically infinite possibilities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, it relies on similar principles, like quantum uncertainty and the many-worlds interpretation. If you like this sort of thing than I recommend Mr. Nobody, or The Garden of Forking Paths, which was written in the 50's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sounds a bit like this from the Rick and Morty episode A Rickle in Time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Once I understood what was going on I immensely enjoyed it. I'd have to agree that the wording of the description doesn't really click until you get to the testing log, but I guess that's what concrete examples are for. Part of me wishes this were longer but the other part is glad it doesn't overstay its' welcome.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You did get it, and thanks, fixed. If I can clarify it in any way, I will

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really hard to wrap my head around, particularly because I don't feel like what happens in the test log is what's explained in the description. I mean, I get that it's making two copies of everything near it based on divergent outcomes to what occurs, but… Maybe it's just the esoteric language being used.

Novote, and "gracilis" shouldn't be capitalized.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Said in chat I like this, obviously nothing has changed. Creepy, unsettling and leaves me wanting more.

Solid +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to Weryllium, Sterbai , minmin and more for help with this.

Shortest I've ever written, clocking in at just under 850 words.

Alternate working title, The Garden of Forking Paths, as an homage to the story by Jorge Luis Borges

+
-

This anomaly is related to why there is no canon ;)

picture
